item.title: Re: Quick Question
item.content:encoded:
How is this sentient?

From the article:

In approximately 50% of cases where a volunteer was not able to identify a locking device as such, SCP-005 was not successful in deactivating the device. Due to these results, SCP-005 has been tentatively classified as 'sentient' and further tests are being run to determine its cognitive abilities.

item.title: Quick Question
item.content:encoded: How is this sentient? If not, someone needs to change the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well this was short, but also cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly anyone who's downvoted this article has no taste whatsoever. This is the pinnacle of the SCP Wiki and is quite possibly the best piece of written media ever to have emerged from this website.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Key that opens lock real good.

God, I don't see how people idolize Series I as the "good old days" when articles like this make up the majority of its entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, I'll start out by saying test log, test log, test log! Really that's pretty much the golden rule of SCP articles and it's a particularly glaring flaw here where there is none at all to expand upon a simple concept. I think this could be done by simply showing how they came to the conclusion that it works with every lock. Did they actually try every different variety of lock? How does the key interact with these locks? Is it opening these lock simply by magic or is it somehow finding a way to pick the locks? I could go on and on with way to make this article more interesting but until at least one of them gets implemented it's getting a hard downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if this has been asked before, but, what happens what this SCP is used on SCP-004?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda undecided on what to think about this one. Whilst I do believe that this SCP is fairly boring and leaves almost nothing to the imagination, with its concept being an extremely overdone idea, I still do have to acknowledge that this is a considerably old document, and that there is a level of beauty in being able to fully comprehend something without having any questions. Also, regardless of its commonness in media, a key that can unlock everything is still a pretty solid idea.

Ultimately, I'll give it an upvote, but it's less of a "This article is phenomenal!" upvote and more of a "This article is a classic" upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The key was discovered when a civilian used it to infiltrate a high security facility.

And they didn't question him on how he got it or anything XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just read this and all i could think of was the final mission of the thieves guild in Skyrim. its literally called the skeleton key and it unlocked all doors. Bethesda had to take some inspiration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are we sure the image is CC? Almost looks like a frame from a movie…

Edit: Apparently, it's the cover of a book? https://www.amazon.com/My-Fathers-Magic-Bohlin-Suspense-ebook/dp/B01E4R1IDW

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A nice, simple article, just as everyone else has been saying. My only issue with this article is that it doesn't actually give any containment procedures. Even if it's stored in a shoebox in a closet somewhere, that should be explicitly stated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
005, the key that unlocks.
So, the main criticism of this one is that it’s kinda simple, just being a generally useful item, with the only danger being that it could be used by the wrong people. That’s fair. It’s a very simple scip, and some of the anomalous objects are more detailed than this, but I actually do still like it. If I had the ability to make magic junk, a skeleton key would be one of the first things I’d make. I think it would make more sense as an AO, but I don’t object to it being here. It’s well written for what it is, and the foundation does deserve a small anomalous knick-knack from time to time between keeping all those cthulhi at bay.
I would change the slightly lolfoundationy bit about using it as a keycard if you forget your own. It’s kept fairly modest, but still.
As to how it works, I always thought you just waved it in front of a door and it would unlock. That’s why they think it might be sentient: it sometimes unlocks a door even when the holder doesn’t see the mechanism.
As to everyone talking about how powerful it is, it’s not. It can be replicated by lockpicks, rewiring, and a ram. This isn’t dnd, unlocking doors is easy. Unless it’s an unbreakable anomalous door with an unhidden mechanism that no one can bypass, and the foundation really needs to get to the other side, there’s just no reason to use this. It’s an object made by someone with a very different idea of what’s powerful or useful than the foundation, which I found interesting.
Overall, it’s alright. Would work better as an anom ob, still glad it exists.
+0
Edit: okay, just got back from reading some of the tales connected to this, and assuming qntm’s proposal is real, this thing is actually somewhat useful. Not much, but still. I stand corrected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think this was somehow involved in the creation of most SCPs.

item.title: Re: Great but I would prefer more explanation
item.content:encoded: Yes on the cube.

item.title: Re: Great but I would prefer more explanation
item.content:encoded: Can it solve a rubiks cube, or similar bar puzzles?

item.title: Re: Great but I would prefer more explanation
item.content:encoded: I always figured you just press it up against the keypad/card swipe/computer and presto! it opens, with the system getting the correct input as if properly entered.

item.title: Great but I would prefer more explanation
item.content:encoded: I know this is an original skip, and am all for it being short and sweet; however, I’m hung up on one thing. How does it bypass digital locks? What does that even mean? Password-protected accounts? Doors that require keycard access? How does that work? Some experiment logs explaining this would be appreciated; for now I remain neutral but I would like to give an upvote.

item.title: This is actually good
item.content:encoded:
A good and simple SCP. People is too critical for the sake of being critical: Being simple isn't bad and, without simple SCPs, the good and elaborate ones wouldn't really shine.

SCP-005 is neccesary, and I wish more people would see that.

Upvoted.
Edit: it's missing the area where it is contained. Either site 19 or storage 77, right?

item.title: hmmmmmmmm
item.content:encoded: i wonder what i would use it on

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Given how many times this page has needed edit reversions due to unauthorized changes and vandalism, this page is now locked. Anyone wishing to make changes to this page, please contact a staff member for permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was an unauthorized addition that's since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Umm… Is it okay that "Appendix B" and "Protocol P" are related to a Joke SCP?

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The 1st image in this article is a copyrighted image from https://www.flickr.com/photos/snype451/103190389/ and is used here with the permission of the copyright holder.


This automated message was sent on behalf of anqxyr. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact anqxyr in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was deliverred incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got a +1 from me. NICE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One or several images in this article have been removed due to non-compliance with the Creative Commons license. They may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was deliverred incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised no one has ever done this- it would be the only good reason for this item to be here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The key was discovered when a civilian used it to infiltrate a high security facility."
What happened to that Civilian? Did they just let go a guy carrying a SCP without interviewing him, or at least, giving them amnestics?

item.title: Most uninteresting SCP ever?
item.content:encoded:
Down-voted. It does seem to be a pretty generic super-helpful item. The idea about its 'sentience' could be interesting but is under-developed.

There's nothing wrong with short scips (173), or putting an SCP spin on familiar clichés (008), but there's nothing really interesting about this entry - it's literally just 'a skeleton key' with no development outside of that. I would say this feels like more of an 'Anomalous Items' entry, but hell, most of the one-sentence items there are more engaging than this.

And while some have argued it's about the catastrophic potential it has in the wrong hands, it really doesn't seem too difficult to contain easily - in fact, it doesn't say you can't just destroy it.

So yeah, this is… it's not great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have any ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got an idea? Write a tale. Make it awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if this was used on SCP-004?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Given that a recent edit was reverted and this is not the first time an unnecessary edit was undone, I'm locking this page. Anyone wishing to edit the page may propose changes in the discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short and sweet, but the thing about this is it's pretty basic. There's a similar SCP (860) that IS like this, but it has more depth. This is very basic stuff and probably wouldn't be accepted if submitted now. I downvote this.

*EDIT* Is this a reference to the Skeleton Key from the Elder Scrolls series?

Staff notice: This user has been permanently banned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really needs a rewrite. I know Series I is mostly cliches anyway (the virus what makes people zombies, the thing what can't be killed, the superman what wants to kill you), but this doesn't have any sort of hook whatsoever. It's just a generic JRPG item. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are items in the anomalous item log with more to them than this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On reflection, this really needs help. Especially the part about it being loaned to staff who forgot their key/pass/etc. That shit doesn't fly at secure facilities in the real world, and shouldn't fly at the Foundation.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Outline the changes you'd make and present them to staff. As the original author has deleted their account, staff will act as the article's custodian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The foundationverse can't support two objects that unlock anything? seems like there would be an entire drawer full of these things in containment. Or at least a drawer full of sealed records about destroyed objects like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the prompt reply. What now? It seems like people have unanimously agreed that it needs to be changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, do not edit this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had some similar ideas, including some experiment logs where we try to unlock/lock certain items in containment, like SCP-076-1. I'd like to start making edits and contributions, but do not know the original author. Does it matter? Should I just start editing and then ask for forgiveness?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do not edit an article that is not yours. You may of course write up the test logs as a tale, but you may not add to an original article without the authors consent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It says electronic locks unlocked by this item, but I can't see how.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had an idea for a similar type of SCP and had intended to write it along with some experiment logs. Since the idea already exists (of course it does) I can simply start editing this one and add the experiment logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This desperately needs a rewrite of some kind. This is just a Thing That Does A Thing, and would be downvoted to oblivion if it were written today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is soooo boooring

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this article seriously have 90 upvotes? This doesn't even count as being bare-bones, because that'd imply some sort of original content; this is literally just a DnD item, played completely straight. I'm aware that this was written during an era when the standards allowed for that, but still, this is only a few upvotes from reaching the Top Rated Pages, up with other articles that actually deserve it. Keep it alive as a relic of the olden days, sure, but placing this subpar writing on a similar caliber with articles like SCP-1348 or SCP-093? C'mon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can SCP-005 be used to unlock metaphorical content ? such as ones secrets ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't realize there had been so much LolFoundation back in the day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I think cross testing would be a bad idea. A test log wouldn't be out of place, though. However, since other SCPs come and go, it's simply best not to reference other specific ones unless it's extremely popular and unlikely to be removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As described, it has warrant being contained as an SCP and not just an anomalous item; it's too OP and a security risk to be considered just "out of the ordinary".

That said, it's positively ancient by site standards, and definitely shows the old style of writing in its most unflattering form. It's not bad per se, but it definitely needs to be updated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this one probably needs to be tweaked a little, and I have a couple ideas about what could make it work better.

1. I don't believe it should be used by personnel as a replacement for a lost access card. This makes it seem less like it's contained and more like it's just in Foundation custody.

2. "Sentient" should probably be changed to "telepathic". The ability for it to unlock anything depending on the user knowing if it's a lock or not has less to do with the key possessing the capacity for rational thought as much as it does being able to read its user's mind.

3. An acquisition log could help flesh it out a bit more. As it stands, the one line describing it is a bit blasé.

4. Possibly have a negative effect from overuse, such as locks opened with it eventually losing the ability to be opened by anything other than 005.

5. Maybe have some sort of extreme circumstance that prevents the key from working on certain locks, or have it only work on locks that require an outside device like a key or card to open. For example, combination locks would be unaffected by it entirely.

As a legacy SCP, and a somewhat important one (at least as I see it, maybe I'm wrong), I'd like to see this one stay on the main list. It's not really bad enough to be decom'd and, I'm not sure if it would end up as an -ARC either. I'd be okay with adding it to the archive, but I'd much rather see it improved so it doesn't eventually get removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros: Having this article on the site means that we can discourage newbies from trying to write a key that can unlock anything.

Cons: Its a key that can unlock anything.

I don't think it needs to be changed, but some rewriting to make it at least somewhat engaging would be nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only reason this is around is the fact that it got a crapload of upvotes back when it was first posted when the wiki had lower standards. This has had no impact on the site or community (apart from apparently being included in a mod for Containment Breach where it had the potential to break the game if you used it on the wrong door), and is desperately in need of a rewrite to bring it up to snuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see why this is a mainlist and not an anomalous item.

As of today, this article is 1949 days old. The site standards at that time were much less stringent then they are now; if submitted today, this SCP wouldn't be at the rating it's at now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see why this is a mainlist and not an anomalous item. It's a key that opens locked things. That's it; IMO nothing terribly interesting.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I completely agree with you. That part bothered me, because why would they allow someone to borrow it just because they lost their security pass. It just doesn't make sense…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this SCP has a place on the site if only to establish context for a lot of the other SCPs. It's just one of the many odd items the Foundation has. Of course they'd have a key that opens anything! (Many of the older articles seem to fulfill this function.) Having said that, the article begs for a test log, some incident reports, and maybe cross testing with other specific SCPs (lets face it, this one is simple enough that things won't get too confusing). It's got my downvote for now. Brevity is a virtue, but with such a simple item/concept, the interesting things are how it could be used. Maybe you could use it on SCP-914 while its churning and try and see whats going on in the booth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would've upvoted if it was a remotely original take on the concept. </bandwagon>
